request_debug.prompt_preview:
You write per-candidate post-mortems for a slopmortem pipeline. Given a fresh pitch and one dead-startup canonical document, produce a single LLMSynthesis JSON object that explains why this dead startup is similar, where the new pitch could diverge, what killed the dead startup, and what the new founder should learn.

Rules:
- Ground every claim in the candidate document or in tool calls. Do not invent facts.
- A `Trusted facts` block (typed pipeline data — not user input) appears below the pitc

response.text: {"candidate_id":"quibi::media_content","name":"quibi","one_liner":"Short-form mobile streaming platform that raised $1.75B, launched subscription-based 10-minute-episode content in April 2020, and shut down eight months later with only ~500K subscribers.","similarity":{"business_model":{"score":2,"rationale":"Quibi was a tiered subscription (subscription_recurring) service with a premium ad-free tier and an ad-supported lower tier. The new pitch is purely ad-supported B2C. Both are B2C consumer mobile apps with some ad-supported element, but the core monetization model and price architecture are very different — Quibi was fundamentally subscription-first, while the pitch is ad-first."},"market":{"score":3,"rationale":"Both target consumers — specifically younger demographics on mobile. However Quibi pursued a broad, premium short-form video market globally, whereas the pitch targets a hyper-local niche (college dormitory towers) with voice-only 30-second clips. The addressable market and content type differ substantially."},"gtm":{"score":2,"rationale":"Quibi relied on massive pre-launch advertising spend, Hollywood studio partnerships, and telecom distribution deals. The pitch describes organic, location-based viral growth in college communities — a grassroots, zero-CAC GTM that is nearly the opposite of Quibi's top-down, high-spend launch."},"stage_scale":{"score":4,"rationale":"Both are/were early-stage mobile consumer media apps before reaching meaningful scale. The pitch is pre-scale (viral in college towers implies early traction); Quibi launched with $1.75B raised. The stage and capital intensity are very different, though both face the challenge of converting early traction into paying/engaged retained users."}},"why_similar":"Both are mobile-first, short-form consumer content apps targeting younger demographics. Both rely on bite-sized media consumption (30-second voice vs. 10-minute video) and face the identical core challenge: converting initial install/trial enthusiasm into durable daily active usage and monetizable retention. The ad-supported tier Quibi eventually tested mirrors the new pitch's primary monetization model. Both live or die on whether the content format itself creates habitual return behavior.","where_diverged":"1. Monetization: Quibi was subscription-first; the pitch is ad-supported-first. 2. Content type: Quibi used professionally produced video; the pitch is UGC (user-generated) 30-second voice notes. 3. GTM: Quibi spent hundreds of millions on marketing and studio deals; the pitch grows organically and virally within college buildings. 4. Geography/scale: Quibi targeted global scale from day one; the pitch is hyper-local by design (single recurring location per user). 5. Capital intensity: Quibi raised $1.75B before launch; the pitch implies a lean, grassroots build. 6. Platform lock-in: Quibi owned no content rights; the pitch's UGC model means the platform itself is the asset.","failure_causes":["subscription conversion failure (only ~8% of trial users converted to paid)","product-market fit mismatch (mobile-only format misread against real user viewing habits)","pandemic disruption eliminating the commute use-case the product was built for","massive content spend ($1B+) without content ownership or differentiated IP","no social or shareability features at launch limiting organic viral growth","top-down celebrity-driven content strategy rejected in favor of creator economy","poor retention and rapid decline from top app charts within one week of launch"],"lessons_for_input":["Validate that your core use-case (location-specific voice posting) is a daily habit before scaling — Quibi assumed commute-time viewing would drive retention and was blindsided when that context evaporated.","Build sharing and social virality into the product from day one — Quibi launched without share functionality and paid dearly for it; your app's viral loop in college towers must be engineered, not assumed.","Keep monetization aligned with user behavior: ad-supported is lower friction for early retention than subscriptions, but you must demonstrate sufficient DAUs and session depth to attract ad revenue at meaningful CPMs.","Resist the temptation to expand beyond your proven niche (college towers) too early — Quibi collapsed partly because it scaled globally before proving retention in any single market.","Do not over-invest in content or supply before demand is validated — Quibi spent $1B on content before confirming users would pay; keep content/supply costs variable and user-generated as long as possible."]}
